title: __init__ and __new__

overview.what: When creating an object in Python, `__new__` is called first to allocate memory and return an uninitialized instance. `__init__` is then called on that instance to initialize its state. Most developers only use `__init__`. You only need `__new__` when you need to control the creation of a new instance (like returning an existing one for a Singleton) or subclassing immutable types like `tuple` or `str`. It is like building a house: `__new__` lays the foundation and frames the structure, while `__init__` paints the walls and moves the furniture in.
overview.why: Understanding the two-step instantiation process allows you to implement advanced patterns like Singletons, flyweight objects, and custom metaclasses.

why_learning_this: Allows implementation of the Singleton pattern.
Enables subclassing immutable types like str or tuple.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting to return the instance in __new__
common_mistakes.explanation: If `__new__` doesn't return an instance of the class, `__init__` is never called. Always return `super().__new__(cls)`.

common_mistakes.title: Trying to return a value from __init__
common_mistakes.explanation: `__init__` must return `None`. It modifies the instance in-place. If you want to return a different object, do it in `__new__`.

recall_questions.q: Which method is responsible for actually creating and returning a new instance in memory?
If `__new__` does not return an instance of the current class, what happens to `__init__`?
recall_questions.answer: It is not called at all.

recall_questions.q: What is the primary reason you would override `__new__` instead of `__init__`?
recall_questions.answer: To control the creation of a new instance, such as returning an existing one (Singleton pattern) or subclassing immutable types.

practice_tasks.title: Subclassing float
practice_tasks.prompt: The provided code subclasses  loat but doesn't change its behavior. Modify it by overriding __new__ so that any value passed to PositiveFloat is converted to its absolute value before initialization.
practice_tasks.starter_code:
class PositiveFloat(float):
    pass

p = PositiveFloat(-3.14)
print(p)

aha_moment.why: Because `__new__` intercepts the creation step, it can return an already-existing object. Here, it returns the same instance for both `a` and `b`, making `a is b` true.

challenge.title: Implementing a basic Singleton
challenge.prompt: Implement a `DatabaseConnection` class using the Singleton pattern. It should have an `__init__` method that sets `self.connected = True` ONLY if the connection hasn't been established yet. Overriding `__new__` will let you ensure only one instance is ever created.

understanding_checks.question: What will this code print when executed?
understanding_checks.why: Python first calls __new__ which allocates the instance and returns it, and since the instance returned is of type Custom, Python immediately calls __init__.

understanding_checks.question: What will this code print when executed?
understanding_checks.why: Because __new__ does not explicitly return the newly created instance, it implicitly returns None. Since it didn't return an instance of MissingReturn, __init__ is never called.
